title: Red Wine on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Red wine on upholstery is the same tannin chemistry as on fabric — anthocyanin polyphenols bond to the fiber through grip. The extra complication on upholstery is construction: padding + batting underneath absorbs pooled liquid, and furniture is not removable-for-extraction the way a garment is. Upholstery codes (W / S / W-S / X) matter: W-code fabrics take water-based cleaning, S-code take solvent-only, W-S take either, X-code take only vacuuming. Check the tag before any liquid touches the fabric.
whyThisWorks: Same principle as carpet: extraction beats chemistry. Damp cloth transfer, never pour. Cold blot flushes what water can carry. Acid-side tannin spotter disrupts the anthocyanin-fiber grip in a localized way. 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes residual color. Weighted dry towel between every wet pass pulls moisture UP toward the surface before it saturates the batting. Upholstery that gets flooded dries with visible water rings AND can wick tannin color back up as it dries.
defaultAssumption: Check the upholstery code tag first (typically under a cushion or on a loose cushion tag). W or W-S: proceed with water-based. S-only: solvent treatment only (not a DIY job). X-code: vacuum only; any liquid will ring or stain the fabric. Assume W or W-S unless the tag says otherwise or there's no tag.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Clean white cotton towel, firm pressure
spottingProtocol.instruction: Find and read the upholstery code tag first. W or W-S: water-based chemistry OK. S-only: stop and use a dry-cleaning solvent instead. X-code: vacuum only — don't attempt liquid removal. Then press a folded white towel straight down on the stain with firm pressure, lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work outside-in. Never rub.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD in cold water, damp cloth, weighted dry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply cold NSD via a DAMP cloth — press onto the stain, lift, rotate cloth. Never pour liquid on upholstery. Immediately press a dry folded towel on top, weighted with a book or heavy pan, for 5 minutes to pull moisture back UP before it reaches the batting. Repeat 2–4 cycles.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — acid-side tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply acid-side tannin spotter by dropper directly to the stain. Never pour. Dwell 5–10 min. Blot with dry towel. Test a hidden patch first on any dyed or patterned upholstery — acid can lift some dyes.
3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop on residual color. Check every 5 minutes for color break. Blot with dry towel immediately when color lifts. One oxidizer at a time; NSD-damp flush before any second pass.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD damp cloth, heavy dry towel, book or pan weight
spottingProtocol.instruction: NSD-damp cloth to clear spotter and oxidizer residue. Then a folded DRY towel on the spot with heavy weight for 30 minutes. This step pulls moisture up from the batting before the upholstery dries — skip it and you'll get a water ring as the fabric dries uneven.
Fan the spot. Air-dry fully before sitting on it again. Inspect the next day for wicking — if a faint ring returns, the batting is saturated and a professional extraction is needed.

steps: 1. Read upholstery code tag. W/W-S: proceed. S-only: dry solvent. X: vacuum only.
3. Cold NSD via damp cloth + weighted dry towel between every pass.
4. Acid-side tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter / PRENETT-A) by dropper. 5–10 min.
5. 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop on residual. 5–15 min, check every 5.
6. NSD-damp flush + 30 min weighted dry towel to pull moisture up from batting.
7. Fan-dry. Inspect next day for wicking.
products: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A)
Absorbent white towels + weight for extraction
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — hot water accelerates tannin browning + can set
warnings: Never pour or pool liquid on upholstery — batting saturation = water ring
Never use hot water — sets tannin brown
Read code tag first — S-only and X-code upholstery can't take water-based treatment
Never skip weighted extraction between wet passes
One oxidizer at a time, NSD-flushed between

homeSolutions.agent: Find the Upholstery Code Tag First
homeSolutions.instruction: Before anything else, find the care tag — usually under a cushion or on a loose label. It'll say W (water-based OK), W-S (either), S (solvent only, not for home), or X (vacuum only, no liquid). W or W-S: proceed. S or X: stop and call a professional. Flooding an S- or X-code fabric with water will ring the fabric permanently, which is a worse outcome than the wine.

homeSolutions.agent: No Alkaline Cleaners on Red Wine
homeSolutions.instruction: Red wine is a tannin. Alkaline products — ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean, most "upholstery cleaner" sprays — LOCK the tannin into the fabric as a permanent brown stain. Use ONLY: cold water, Dawn dish soap, and 3% hydrogen peroxide. Salt and club soda don't help much either — what actually works is blot-extract and the Dawn/peroxide chemistry.

Press a thick stack of white paper towels or a folded white cloth straight down on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work outside the stain toward the center. Never rub — rubbing drives wine into the batting underneath, where you can't reach it from the surface.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water, Small Amounts — Damp Cloth Only
homeSolutions.instruction: Dampen a white cloth with cold water (wrung nearly dry). Press it on the spot, lift, rotate. Repeat. DO NOT pour water on upholstery — it goes straight through to the batting and rings the fabric permanently as it dries.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + 3% Hydrogen Peroxide — Dabbed, Not Poured
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon clear Dawn dish soap with 1 cup 3% hydrogen peroxide. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, dab the stain — edges toward center. Let sit 5 minutes, blot with a clean damp cloth. Peroxide oxidizes the wine pigment; Dawn lifts it. Repeat 2–3 cycles.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with ammonia or chlorine bleach. Skip this step entirely on dark, patterned, or delicate upholstery — test a hidden patch first.

homeSolutions.agent: Weighted Dry Towel Between Every Wet Pass
homeSolutions.instruction: After each wet dab pass, press a thick DRY towel on the spot and weight it with a heavy book or pan for 20–30 minutes. This pulls moisture UP out of the batting before it can dry into a water ring. This step is the difference between upholstery that dries clean and upholstery that dries with a visible halo.

Point a fan at the spot. Don't sit on the furniture until it's bone-dry — several hours. Inspect the next day in daylight. If a faint pink ring returns, call a professional upholstery cleaner. Home chemistry has plateaued at that point.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for S-Code, Wicking, or Plateau
homeSolutions.instruction: Call a pro if: the upholstery code is S or X, the ring wicked back as it dried (batting saturated), the test patch showed dye bleed, or two full home rounds didn't clear it. Tell them: "Red wine on [W / W-S / code unknown] upholstery, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide + weighted dry towel. [Ring wicked back / plateau / no tag / dye bled]."

steps: 1. Find the upholstery code tag. W or W-S: proceed. S or X: stop, call a pro.
2. NO alkaline cleaners (ammonia, OxiClean, baking soda) on red wine — locks tannin brown.
3. Blot straight down from outside to center. Never rub.
4. Cold water with a DAMP cloth — never pour.
5. Dawn + 3% peroxide solution, dab with damp cloth, 5 min, 2–3 cycles. Patch-test first.
6. Weighted dry towel 20–30 min between every wet pass.
7. Fan-dry. Inspect next day for wicking.
8. Escalate for S-code, wicking, or plateau.
products: Clean white towels or thick paper towel stack
A heavy book or pan for weighted drying
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if upholstery code is S or X, if the stain wicks back as it dries, if patch test shows dye bleed, or if two home rounds don't clear it. Call a professional upholstery cleaner.
warnings: NEVER pour or pool liquid on upholstery — saturates batting, rings fabric
NEVER use alkaline cleaners on tannin — locks as permanent brown
NEVER skip weighted extraction between wet passes
NEVER mix peroxide with ammonia or chlorine bleach
S-code and X-code upholstery are NOT DIY — call a pro

neverDo: Never pour or pool liquid on upholstery — batting saturates
Never use alkaline cleaners on red wine tannin — permanent brown
Never ignore the upholstery code tag — S-code damages under water
Never skip extraction between wet passes
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time
Never sit on wet upholstery — drives moisture deeper into batting
fiberSensitivities: W / W-S code upholstery tolerates water-based chemistry at room temperature
S-code: solvent only — water causes permanent rings
X-code: vacuum only — no liquid permitted
Delicate / silk / velvet upholstery: escalate even on W-code

materialWarnings: Read the upholstery code before any liquid
Never pool — damp cloth transfer only

escalation.when: Upholstery code is S or X, stain wicks back after drying, patch test shows dye bleed, upholstery is silk/velvet/delicate, or two home rounds haven't cleared it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Red wine on [W / W-S / code unknown] upholstery, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + 3% H₂O₂ + weighted dry towel. [Ring wicked back / plateau / no tag / dye bled.]"

contextQuestions: What does the upholstery code tag say — W, W-S, S, or X?
How long ago did the spill happen?
Has anything alkaline (OxiClean, ammonia) been applied already?
Is the upholstery delicate fabric (silk, velvet) or durable (synthetic, cotton blend)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: S-code or X-code = pro-lane by default. Alkaline already applied = permanent brown, replacement-only. W-code + fresh + no alkaline = good DIY odds. Silk / velvet upholstery = escalate regardless of code.
